medical.title: An experimental molecule 'reprograms' the brain's defenses against Alzheimer's disease
medical.summary: A team has identified an experimental molecule capable of "reprogramming" the brain's immune cells to restore part of their protective function against Alzheimer's disease. The study, published in the journal Cell Death and Disease, shows that the compound, called OLE, helps microglia enclose and contain beta-amyloid plaques, reducing their size and toxicity.

medical.title: ‘Imagination and creation are products of time and space’
medical.summary: With arts funding shrinking, Ireland is trying a different approach: paying artists enough to live on. Could Britain do the same? The post ‘Imagination and creation are products of time and space’ appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
medical.summary: A small clinical trial suggests that probiotics may offer a surprising mental health boost for older adults with depression. Seniors who took a daily probiotic alongside their regular antidepressant treatment experienced slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety symptoms than those who received a placebo.

medical.title: Scientists engineer personalized cartilage graft for infants with life-threatening airway narrowing
medical.summary: A study led by researchers at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia (CHOP) demonstrates a new method of using decellularized cartilage with patient-specific cells to help enlarge pediatric airways narrowed as a result of severe subglottic stenosis. Researchers demonstrate that this new method is faster, more effective and able to overcome issues associated with the current standard grafts, such as donor site morbidity, insufficient tissue volume and a delayed timeline. The findings are published in the journal Nature Communications.

medical.title: Probiotics could help treat depression
medical.summary: In a pilot clinical trial published in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society that included older adults with depression receiving standard care, adding probiotic therapy produced modest but meaningful reductions in depressive and anxiety symptoms compared with adding a placebo. However, both groups demonstrated substantial overall improvements during follow-up.

medical.title: Rural–urban differences may exist in use of mental health care for veterans with serious mental illness
medical.summary: An analysis published in The Journal of Rural Health has found that among U.S. veterans with serious mental illness (SMI), rural veterans were somewhat more likely to have co-occurring mental health conditions than urban veterans but were less likely to receive SMI care.

medical.title: A framework for assessing social–emotional skills in youth may be lacking
medical.summary: A recent study in PsyCh Journal has uncovered numerous limitations in applying a popular framework for assessing social–emotional skills (such as empathy, persistence and curiosity) to children and adolescents around the world.

medical.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
medical.summary: A minimally invasive treatment that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee provided significant pain relief and improved function for osteoarthritis patients, with benefits lasting at least a year. The procedure was safe, highly successful, and could offer a new alternative for people seeking relief before considering knee replacement.

science.title: Frozen Greenland middens preserve 4,500 years of farms, seal hunts and toilets
science.summary: Greenland has a long and checkered history of human settlement: several Paleo-Inuit cultures since approximately 2,500 BCE, descendants of Vikings between the 10th and 15th centuries, and early modern Danes since 1721. All left their traces on the landscape, for example in the form of ancient domestic rubbish heaps. Composed of waste like animal bones, excrement, mollusk shells and human artifacts, these middens are a precious resource for archaeologists.

science.title: Heading for the Atlantic Ocean to study the impact of climate change on ocean circulation
science.summary: It is becoming increasingly urgent to study the effects of climate change on the global ocean system. Future projections indicate significant changes in the Mediterranean and Atlantic circulation systems, which could have unpredictable climatic consequences, especially for one of the most influential ocean currents in the global climate: the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC).

science.title: Even morphologically similar pollinators carry distinct pollen assemblages
science.summary: The body size, morphology and associated behavioral traits of flower-visiting insects strongly influence the quantity of pollen they transport. Thus, pollinators with similar appearances are often assumed to exert similar ecological effects on plants. However, pollinators' effects on plants are not determined solely by the amount of pollen they carry.

science.title: Indian millets contain distinct lipid fingerprints with anti-diabetic and anti-inflammatory potential
science.summary: Indian millets are a staple for the country's population of more than a billion. They are also gaining global popularity, with rising exports and a reputation as a climate-resilient crop. Now, new research shows that their nutritional value includes a rich diversity of lipids, including a previously undetected group that may offer potential health benefits. The research is published in the journal Food Chemistry.

science.title: Are alien probes hiding in our backyard? A new study says we've barely looked
science.summary: Even at this early stage in our spacefaring age, humanity has already begun sending probes that will eventually reach other solar systems, even if that was not their original intention. Five robotic explorers—Pioneer 10 and 11, Voyager 1 and 2, and New Horizons—are all on escape trajectories out of the solar system and might someday enter another one. They will no longer be operational at that point, but they serve as proof of concept that spacefaring civilizations do indeed build interstellar probes.

science.title: Switzerland map reveals ground light and shade in 10-meter detail
science.summary: Researchers have calculated light levels at the ground surface across Switzerland to within 10 meters, in both open and forested areas. The model even simulates the shadows cast by individual trees.

science.title: Dark biodiversity helps solve Darwin's 160-year-old puzzle
science.summary: An international research team, which included University of Tartu visiting doctoral student Wen-Gang Zhang and Professor of Botany Meelis Pärtel, has found a new solution to one of ecology's long-standing controversies—Darwin's naturalization conundrum, which addresses the question of why some species successfully establish in a new habitat while others do not. The research is published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

science.title: Ultrasound unlocks protein from cauliflower waste and could add value to existing crops
science.summary: An RMIT University innovation uses ultrasound to extract leaf protein from discarded cauliflower leaves, identifying a potential new use for vegetable scraps. The process could help food manufacturers turn vegetable waste into protein ingredients, reducing waste and adding value to existing crops.

science.title: Rethinking plant photoprotection: New insights into antenna protein CP26
science.summary: Plants must carefully strike a balance between harvesting sunlight to fuel photosynthesis and protecting themselves from light damage. Part of this balancing act is performed by antenna proteins that are bound to light-harvesting molecules inside plant chloroplasts. Mostly known for harvesting energy from sunlight, some antenna proteins can dissipate excess absorbed energy as heat through a photoprotection mechanism called non-photochemical quenching (NPQ).

science.title: Q&A: Boosting NASA's Swift Observatory to support continued space observation
science.summary: NASA's "rapid-response" space telescope is slowly falling out of orbit, but a daring mission this summer could allow the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory to continue scanning the sky for many more years to come. In the first mission of its kind, a spacecraft will launch from Earth and rendezvous with Swift to boost it to a higher altitude and extend its life.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: An experimental molecule 'reprograms' the brain's defenses against Alzheimer's disease
mix_items.summary: A team has identified an experimental molecule capable of "reprogramming" the brain's immune cells to restore part of their protective function against Alzheimer's disease. The study, published in the journal Cell Death and Disease, shows that the compound, called OLE, helps microglia enclose and contain beta-amyloid plaques, reducing their size and toxicity.

mix_items.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
mix_items.summary: A small clinical trial suggests that probiotics may offer a surprising mental health boost for older adults with depression. Seniors who took a daily probiotic alongside their regular antidepressant treatment experienced slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety symptoms than those who received a placebo.

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Scientists engineer personalized cartilage graft for infants with life-threatening airway narrowing
mix_items.summary: A study led by researchers at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia (CHOP) demonstrates a new method of using decellularized cartilage with patient-specific cells to help enlarge pediatric airways narrowed as a result of severe subglottic stenosis. Researchers demonstrate that this new method is faster, more effective and able to overcome issues associated with the current standard grafts, such as donor site morbidity, insufficient tissue volume and a delayed timeline. The findings are published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Probiotics could help treat depression
mix_items.summary: In a pilot clinical trial published in the Journal of the American Geriatrics Society that included older adults with depression receiving standard care, adding probiotic therapy produced modest but meaningful reductions in depressive and anxiety symptoms compared with adding a placebo. However, both groups demonstrated substantial overall improvements during follow-up.

mix_items.title: Rural–urban differences may exist in use of mental health care for veterans with serious mental illness
mix_items.summary: An analysis published in The Journal of Rural Health has found that among U.S. veterans with serious mental illness (SMI), rural veterans were somewhat more likely to have co-occurring mental health conditions than urban veterans but were less likely to receive SMI care.

Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: A framework for assessing social–emotional skills in youth may be lacking
mix_items.summary: A recent study in PsyCh Journal has uncovered numerous limitations in applying a popular framework for assessing social–emotional skills (such as empathy, persistence and curiosity) to children and adolescents around the world.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
mix_items.summary: A minimally invasive treatment that blocks inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee provided significant pain relief and improved function for osteoarthritis patients, with benefits lasting at least a year. The procedure was safe, highly successful, and could offer a new alternative for people seeking relief before considering knee replacement.

mix_items.title: Frozen Greenland middens preserve 4,500 years of farms, seal hunts and toilets
mix_items.summary: Greenland has a long and checkered history of human settlement: several Paleo-Inuit cultures since approximately 2,500 BCE, descendants of Vikings between the 10th and 15th centuries, and early modern Danes since 1721. All left their traces on the landscape, for example in the form of ancient domestic rubbish heaps. Composed of waste like animal bones, excrement, mollusk shells and human artifacts, these middens are a precious resource for archaeologists.

mix_items.title: Heading for the Atlantic Ocean to study the impact of climate change on ocean circulation
mix_items.summary: It is becoming increasingly urgent to study the effects of climate change on the global ocean system. Future projections indicate significant changes in the Mediterranean and Atlantic circulation systems, which could have unpredictable climatic consequences, especially for one of the most influential ocean currents in the global climate: the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC).

mix_items.title: Even morphologically similar pollinators carry distinct pollen assemblages
mix_items.summary: The body size, morphology and associated behavioral traits of flower-visiting insects strongly influence the quantity of pollen they transport. Thus, pollinators with similar appearances are often assumed to exert similar ecological effects on plants. However, pollinators' effects on plants are not determined solely by the amount of pollen they carry.

mix_items.title: Indian millets contain distinct lipid fingerprints with anti-diabetic and anti-inflammatory potential
mix_items.summary: Indian millets are a staple for the country's population of more than a billion. They are also gaining global popularity, with rising exports and a reputation as a climate-resilient crop. Now, new research shows that their nutritional value includes a rich diversity of lipids, including a previously undetected group that may offer potential health benefits. The research is published in the journal Food Chemistry.

mix_items.title: Are alien probes hiding in our backyard? A new study says we've barely looked
mix_items.summary: Even at this early stage in our spacefaring age, humanity has already begun sending probes that will eventually reach other solar systems, even if that was not their original intention. Five robotic explorers—Pioneer 10 and 11, Voyager 1 and 2, and New Horizons—are all on escape trajectories out of the solar system and might someday enter another one. They will no longer be operational at that point, but they serve as proof of concept that spacefaring civilizations do indeed build interstellar probes.

mix_items.title: Switzerland map reveals ground light and shade in 10-meter detail
mix_items.summary: Researchers have calculated light levels at the ground surface across Switzerland to within 10 meters, in both open and forested areas. The model even simulates the shadows cast by individual trees.

mix_items.title: Dark biodiversity helps solve Darwin's 160-year-old puzzle
mix_items.summary: An international research team, which included University of Tartu visiting doctoral student Wen-Gang Zhang and Professor of Botany Meelis Pärtel, has found a new solution to one of ecology's long-standing controversies—Darwin's naturalization conundrum, which addresses the question of why some species successfully establish in a new habitat while others do not. The research is published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

mix_items.title: Ultrasound unlocks protein from cauliflower waste and could add value to existing crops
mix_items.summary: An RMIT University innovation uses ultrasound to extract leaf protein from discarded cauliflower leaves, identifying a potential new use for vegetable scraps. The process could help food manufacturers turn vegetable waste into protein ingredients, reducing waste and adding value to existing crops.

mix_items.title: Rethinking plant photoprotection: New insights into antenna protein CP26
mix_items.summary: Plants must carefully strike a balance between harvesting sunlight to fuel photosynthesis and protecting themselves from light damage. Part of this balancing act is performed by antenna proteins that are bound to light-harvesting molecules inside plant chloroplasts. Mostly known for harvesting energy from sunlight, some antenna proteins can dissipate excess absorbed energy as heat through a photoprotection mechanism called non-photochemical quenching (NPQ).

mix_items.title: Q&A: Boosting NASA's Swift Observatory to support continued space observation
mix_items.summary: NASA's "rapid-response" space telescope is slowly falling out of orbit, but a daring mission this summer could allow the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory to continue scanning the sky for many more years to come. In the first mission of its kind, a spacecraft will launch from Earth and rendezvous with Swift to boost it to a higher altitude and extend its life.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am zis ca fac sport. Am facut: am fugit de responsabilitati.

satire_ro.title: Previzibil! Biserica lui Gigi Becali s-a prăbuşit după ce a intrat o femeie în ea
